metadata.title: Computer science applied graph (gold, true summary semantics)
notes: Hub nodes are structural containers only and are not summary nodes.
Summary nodes should be created only when a mature component can be compressed into one reusable language node.
Several low-confidence hypothesis nodes are intentionally retained so the controller can learn when to keep, refine, or overturn tentative ideas.
False or overgeneralized claims remain in the graph with low confidence to support resolve_conflict supervision.
Branching support patterns are preferred over narrow chains: many claims support multiple downstream consequences or tradeoffs.
Cross-cluster bridge opportunities are intentionally left partially incomplete so create_bridge and link_nodes remain meaningful tasks.
metadata.source_note: Gold supervision graph for routing/edit tasks over databases, networking, graphics, HCI, information retrieval, and compression. Structural hubs are hubs rather than summaries. The graph includes accepted principles, misconceptions, and tentative low-confidence hypotheses to supervise add/update/no_op/resolve decisions under uncertainty.

nodes.text: This graph covers applied computer science topics in data, communication, interaction, media, and retrieval.
Databases study data organization, constraints, querying, and efficient access.
Networking studies communication across machines, protocols, delay, and naming.
Computer graphics studies digital images, rendering, visibility, and sampling.
Human-computer interaction studies usability, feedback, perception, and interface design.
Information retrieval studies search indexes, ranking signals, and relevance tradeoffs.
Compression studies compact data representation with or without exact reconstruction.
Bridge insights connect storage, retrieval, delivery, media, and user experience.
The relational model organizes data into tables with rows and columns.
A primary key uniquely identifies a row in a table.
A foreign key links a row to a key value in another table.
Normalization reduces redundant data across related tables.
Redundant data can cause update anomalies when copies become inconsistent.
An index can speed selective lookups by avoiding a full table scan.
Adding an index always makes every query faster.
A B-tree is a common database index structure used for ordered access.
Packet switching sends data in discrete packets that share network links.
TCP provides reliable, ordered byte delivery between endpoints.
UDP does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or duplicate suppression.
DNS maps domain names to network resource records such as IP addresses.
Latency and bandwidth are different properties of a network path.
Congestion can increase delay and trigger packet loss.
Higher bandwidth automatically means lower latency.
A content delivery network caches content closer to users to reduce response delay.
Raster graphics represent an image as a grid of pixels.
Vector graphics represent images with geometric primitives and formulas.
Aliasing appears when image sampling is too coarse for the visual detail present.
Anti-aliasing reduces jagged edge artifacts created by discrete sampling.
A Z-buffer keeps the closest visible fragment at each screen position.
Texture filtering reduces visual artifacts when sampling images onto surfaces.
Increasing pixel count alone solves aliasing artifacts.
An SVG icon is a typical example of vector graphics.
An affordance signals what action a user can take with an interface element.
Feedback helps users understand the result of their actions.
Larger and nearer targets are generally faster to acquire under Fitts's law.
Consistent interface patterns reduce learning cost and user error.
Usability testing with representative users reveals problems that designers may miss.
Higher contrast often improves legibility for visual text and controls.
Expert review alone is enough to establish good usability.
Highlighting a pressed button is an example of interface feedback.
An inverted index maps each term to a postings list of matching documents.
TF-IDF increases the weight of terms that are informative for distinguishing documents.
Precision is the fraction of retrieved items that are relevant.
Recall is the fraction of relevant items that are retrieved.
Query expansion can improve recall while reducing precision.
Stemming merges related word forms to broaden matching.
Improving recall automatically improves precision.
A search engine term lookup over postings lists is an example of inverted indexing.
Lossless compression preserves all original information for exact reconstruction.
Lossy compression reduces size by discarding some information.
Entropy places a theoretical limit on average lossless compression for a source model.
Huffman coding gives shorter codewords to more frequent symbols.
Run-length encoding works well when the same symbol repeats many times.
PNG is a common example of lossless image compression.
Compression never changes data quality.
JPEG is a common example of lossy image compression.
An inverted index is a specialized indexing structure for text retrieval over document collections.
Network latency directly affects perceived responsiveness in interactive online applications.
Image compression enables faster media delivery by reducing transmitted data size.
Precision and recall tradeoffs shape user experience in search interfaces.
A covering index can answer some queries without fetching full base-table rows.
Additional indexes can speed reads but may slow writes because inserts, deletes, and updates must maintain index entries.
Denormalization can reduce join cost for read-heavy workloads, but it increases redundancy and consistency risk.
Joins on foreign-key relationships can become expensive when supporting indexes are absent.
Denormalization is always a design mistake and should never be used.
Packet loss can reduce TCP throughput because retransmission and congestion control limit the sending rate.
Using multiple streams over QUIC can reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking compared with a single ordered byte stream.
Caching popular responses closer to clients can reduce origin load and improve perceived performance.
Applications can add their own reliability mechanisms above UDP when the transport itself does not guarantee delivery.
Only TCP can be used to build reliable applications.
Mipmaps reduce minification artifacts by sampling from prefiltered lower-resolution texture levels.
Supersampling improves image quality by taking more samples, but it increases computational cost.
Alpha blending can depend on draw order because semi-transparent fragments do not combine like opaque visibility tests.
Raising resolution can reduce visible aliasing, but it does not eliminate sampling problems in general.
Progressive disclosure can reduce cognitive overload by revealing advanced options only when needed.
Interfaces should keep users informed about what the system is doing and whether an action is still in progress.
Excessive latency can disrupt the action-feedback loop and make an interface feel unresponsive even when functionality is correct.
Giving users more visible options always improves usability.
Click data can be a noisy relevance signal because position bias and presentation effects influence what users choose.
Removing stopwords can hurt phrase queries or titles where the omitted words contribute meaning.
BM25 uses diminishing returns for repeated term frequency instead of rewarding every repetition linearly.
Aggressive index pruning can reduce latency or storage but may lower recall by removing low-weight matches.
Transform coding can concentrate signal energy into a smaller set of coefficients that are easier to compress.
Quantization trades bitrate for distortion by coarsening represented values.
Video compression exploits temporal redundancy between nearby frames in addition to spatial redundancy within a frame.
Stronger compression is always better because the smallest file is the best outcome.
